articles.title: Bun v1.3.10 Released: A Surprisingly Big Update
articles.description: Bun’s REPL has been completely rewritten with many improvements (both practical and cosmetic), there's a --compile --target=browser option for building self-contained HTML files with all JS, CSS, and assets included (ideal for simple JS-powered single page apps), full support for TC39 stage 3 ES decorators, a faster event loop, barrel import optimization, and more.
articles.author: Jarred Sumner

articles.title: External Import Maps, Today!
articles.description: Several weeks ago Lea posted about web dependencies being broken, but now she has a solution. The core technique employed to emulate support for external import maps isn't obvious, despite being simple, but is already offered by JSPM 4.0.
articles.author: Lea Verou

articles.title: Making WebAssembly a First-Class Citizen on the Web
articles.description: WASM has come a long way but remains tricky to work with on the Web, with even performing a console.log requiring a lot of glue code. Ryan makes the case that the WebAssembly Component Model could change this by letting modules bind directly to browser APIs, load directly from script tags, and more.
articles.author: Ryan Hunt

articles.title: We Deserve a Better Streams API for JavaScript
articles.description: “I’m publishing this to start a conversation,” says James who shows off an alternative approach to Web streams that works around the current standard’s “fundamental usability and performance issues.” The end results and James' extensive experience in this area make for a compelling argument.
articles.author: James M Snell

articles.title: The Illusion of JavaScript-Powered 'DRM'
articles.description: An explanation of why building a DRM/copy protection system purely in JavaScript (rather than EME-based approaches) is ultimately just “sophisticated friction”, at best, and uses a tale of breaking a (NSFW) platform’s protection to make the point.
articles.author: Ahmed Arat

articles.title: How Cloudflare Rebuilt Next.js with AI in a Week
articles.description: vinext is an experimental, Vite-based reimplementation of Next.js’s API surface, letting existing apps run in more environments, though with some tradeoffs.
articles.author: Steve Faulkner (Cloudflare)

articles.title: Using Val Town to Get Me to the Movies
articles.description: Val Town is a fantastic platform for quickly writing and deploying JavaScript-powered services. Like this one!
articles.author: Raymond Camden

articles.title: txiki.js: A Small, Powerful JavaScript Runtime
articles.description: Stands on the shoulders of QuickJS-ng and libuv and aims to support the latest ECMAScript features while being WinterTC compliant. GitHub repo.
articles.author: Saúl Ibarra Corretgé

articles.title: numpy-ts: A NumPy Implementation for TypeScript
articles.description: A recreation of NumPy, a fundamental piece of the Python scientific computing ecosystem, that works in the browser, Node, Bun, and Deno. 94% of NumPy’s API is covered so far and there’s an online playground to give it a try.
articles.author: Nicolas Dupont

articles.title: Yoopta Editor 6.0: A Headless Rich Text Editor for React
articles.description: MIT-licensed library for creating block-based, Notion-style rich text editing experiences. It’s headless at heart, but comes with a variety of UI components to get started fast. The playground offers a live example.
articles.author: Akhmed Ibragimov

articles.title: AdonisJS v7 Released: 'Batteries-Included' Node.js Framework
articles.description: A popular framework that includes auth, ORM, queues, testing, etc. With v7 comes an all new web site, OpenTelemetry integration, new starter kits to rapidly build new apps, and more.
articles.author: Harminder Virk

articles.title: 🎨 Color Thief 3.0: Grab Color Palettes from Images
articles.description: Given an image, this uses canvas to return a list of the dominant colors. Works in browsers or Node. Now with OKLCH support, Web Worker offloading, ‘live extraction’ for video, canvas and image elements, and more. GitHub repo.
articles.author: Lokesh Dhakar

articles.title: 📊 ng2-charts: Chart.js-Based Charting Library for Angular
articles.description: Now with Angular 20 support.
articles.author: Valor Labs

articles.title: vue-superselect: A Headless Select/Combobox for Vue 3
articles.author: Nemanja Malesija

articles.description: The React Foundation has officially launched taking over ownership of React, React Native and JSX. It has a board of eight founding members (including Meta) with Seth Webster, former head of React at Meta, as exec director.
It's not formal yet, but this preview post reveals how Node.js's release schedule is changing later this year (one major release per year, every release to be LTS, with no more odd/even distinction).
📢 Monthly roundups have landed from Svelte, ViteLand / VoidZero, and Astro.
🔒 The Angular team explains two recently patched vulnerabilities in Angular.
The Navigation API is now Baseline Newly Available across all major browsers.
Deno 2.7 – The alternative runtime stabilizes Temporal API support, supports Windows on ARM, adds package.json overrides support, and more.
Expo SDK 55 – The popular React Native framework/toolchain.
Shiki 4.0 – TextMate grammar powered, VS Code-like syntax highlighter.
Angular 21.2, Mediabunny 1.35, Neo.mjs 12.0

articles.title: The Most-Seen UI on the Internet? Redesigning Turnstile and Challenge Pages
articles.description: We’ve all seen Cloudflare’s verification widget, or challenge pages — heck, you may well have seen them already today. Cloudflare serves these over 7.5 billion times each day, so making any design changes requires a lot of care. This is a detailed look at how they do just that, while maintaining accessibility and security standards.
articles.author: Bacevicius, Foppa, Elmore (Cloudflare)

articles.title: Chrome is Moving to a Two-Week Release Cycle
articles.description: Starting in September, in an effort “to match the demands of a modern web”, Chrome will shift from a four-week to a two-week release cadence. This change is to the stable release, with no changes announced for the Dev and Canary channels. Chrome 200 in time for the next Olympics?
articles.author: Mason & Ravichandran (Chrome for Developers)

articles.title: Why is WebAssembly a Second-Class Language on the Web?
articles.description: WASM may well have come a long way, but it remains tricky to work with huge amounts of glue code par for the course. Ryan makes the case that the WebAssembly Component Model could change this, by letting modules bind directly to browser APIs, load directly from script tags, and more.
articles.author: Ryan Hunt

articles.title: You Can Use Newline Characters (and Tabs) in URLs
articles.description: Daniel clearly explains some of the nuances of the WHATWG URL spec, revealing that browsers essentially allow for both newline and tab characters within URLs. Yes, it’s not actually valid, but it does work — meaning you don’t have to have unreadable long strings. Not something I was aware of!
articles.author: Daniel Lemire

articles.title: Getting Started with the Popover API
articles.description: The Popover API turns tooltips from something you simulate, into something the browser actually understands — and this is a good primer on using it.
articles.author: Godstime Aburu

articles.title: An Exploit ... in CSS?!
articles.description: The vulnerability highlighted here (the first major zero-day exploit of 2026) revolves around Chromium’s handling of the CSS @font-feature-values rule, but it’s actually triggered via JavaScript. Lee examines it, and encourages us to update our browsers.
articles.author: Lee Meyer

articles.title: The Odometer Effect (Without JavaScript)
articles.description: Preethi shows us how to approach creating a dynamic display for showing changing numeric values with just CSS and HTML.
articles.author: Preethi Sam

articles.title: Chip Away
articles.description: A critique of an often overused design pattern (badges or lozenges with small text), pointing out its flaws, and suggesting what we should do instead.
articles.author: Donnie D'Amato

articles.title: ▶  Trying the New CodePen 2.0
articles.description: We’re big fans of CodePen, so it’s great to see it go from strength to strength with a slew of new features and a complete rewrite of the platform. Cassidy runs through the changes here, including a new file system, tabbed views, code processors, and more. It’s currently in private beta, but you can request an invite.
articles.author: Cassidy Williams

articles.title: ASCII Drawing Board: A Web-Based Tool to Draw ASCII Diagrams
articles.description: Has a few cool features including a “brush” of three sizes, custom sized canvas, and your text “brushes” are saved for later use.
articles.author: Denis

articles.title: Swatchify: Extract Dominant Colors from Images
articles.description: Available as a Go-based CLI, a JS library, and REST API. Works entirely in the browser and has no dependencies. Accepts 6 different image formats and outputs as text, JSON, or PNG palette. Repo here.
articles.author: James Campbell

articles.title: 8bitcn/ui: A Set of 50+ Accessible Retro-Styled UI Components
articles.description: For that pixelated look your 1980s inner child will love! The platform is also a shadcn/ui registry so you can easily add your components, and you can enable an MCP server for access to the library via your IDE. Code here.
articles.author: OrcDev

articles.title: Heatmap: A Diff Viewer for Code Reviews
articles.description: Replace “github․com” in any pull request URL with 0github․com and see a color-coded diff of each line/token indicating how much human attention the PR needs.
articles.author: cmux

articles.title: 1995: From Batman Forever’s Cinematic Design to HTML Tables
articles.description: By 1995, the web had become a destination rather than just a document repository — as highlighted by this look back at the highly visual (for the time) Batman Forever website.
articles.author: Richard MacManus
